name: Silas Weir
whyHere: You finished something. Completely. It nearly got you exiled, and you are still not sure they were wrong.

inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Belt Knife
inventory.description: A plain working blade — more tool than weapon, but it has been both.

inventory.name: Hemp Rope (30 ft)
inventory.description: Tarred hemp, knotted every arm's length. The difference between a fall and a climb.

inventory.name: Preserved Trail Parcel
inventory.description: Aldric's smokehouse goods — dried river perch and salted pork, cloth-wrapped and knotted. Holds a week on the road. Two parcels.

deeds.description: Raised a cleaned rabbit from the dead in the upper meadow — no ritual, no preparation, hands and will and the valley's old grain answering
Struck a fair provisioning arrangement with Aldric the smokehouse man — hunting for the village in exchange for food and supplies, and offered to keep watch on what's damaging the river catch.

turns.summary: Silas arrives in Millbrook at first light — the river runs gray-green, the villagers are quiet, and the weight of the valley's unresolved crisis hangs over everything.
turns.narration:
The Echo River is the first thing you notice — or rather, what's wrong with it. You came down into Millbrook along the eastern track before full light, and the water caught the early sun in a way clean water shouldn't: a faint greenish cast, like verdigris on old copper, drifting in slow wreaths from the upstream bend. By the time you reached the dock, a fisherman was already pulling in his nets early, jaw set, not talking about why.

Millbrook wakes with the kind of noise that means people are pretending harder than usual. The water-wheel at the north mill turns steadily; children are shouting somewhere up the terraced gardens; a cart horse stands patient outside the cooperage. The village well — a broad stone collar near the market square — has a small knot of people around it, three women and an old man, speaking low. One of them glances at the river and then doesn't again. You are a stranger here, pack still on your back, dust of the road still on your boots, and nobody has looked at you twice yet except for a carrion bird the size of a small cat that has settled on the dock-post beside you and is regarding the morning with the calm of something that has nowhere better to be.

Huginn shifts its weight once, unhurried, and makes a sound low in its chest — not a croak, more of a consideration. The gray-green tinge in the water catches a shaft of light and, just for a moment, looks almost organized: a slow pulse rather than a drift. Then it's gone, just discolored water again, and a child runs past the dock chasing a rolling hoop and pays the river no attention at all.

You know, from talk on the road these last days, that Millbrook's elders have formally named what everyone else is still tiptoeing around — a Precursor system, operating, beneath the valley. You know someone named Fendt has been marked, publicly, on a board in the Radiant Plateau's Edge District, and that the ledger containing three weeks of falsified water readings under that name has since gone missing. The gap between what is known and what anyone is doing about it is wide and getting wider. This village is at the bottom of that gap. And you are standing in it, on the first morning of spring, with a knife at your belt and a bird at your shoulder and no particular reason anyone here should listen to you yet.

turns.summary: Silas scouts the countryside north of Millbrook with Huginn's guidance, takes two rabbits and a pigeon, and notices the discoloration holds its shape unnaturally in the upstream bend.
turns.narration:
You leave the dock behind while the village is still rubbing sleep from its eyes, following the Echo River's bank north until the terraced gardens thin out and the valley opens into scrubland and wet meadow. The ground is soft with snowmelt — boot-prints hold their shape for a moment before the soil swallows them. Spring is only a rumor here: a few yellow shoots poking through last year's brown mat, willows along the bank just beginning to redden at the tips.

Huginn lifts off your shoulder without ceremony and climbs in a wide, unhurried spiral above the riverside scrub. It is not hunting — it cannot — but it reads the land like a warden reads a ledger: a tilt of wing over the brushy hollow where something moved, a slow banking circle that draws your eye to the game trail cutting through the sedge toward the higher meadow. You follow its arcs the way you'd follow a pointing finger. Three times it hovers just long enough over a spot that you find fresh tracks — rabbit, deer-slot, the pressed grass of a resting hare. The bird never once looks pleased with itself.

By mid-morning you've worked the hollow and the upper meadow both. The hunting is honest if not spectacular: two rabbits, snared and dispatched at the trail junction where Huginn circled longest, and a fat wood-pigeon that had no idea what it was resting next to. You break and clean them at the meadow's edge, the birds' warmth fading fast in the cold air. The Belt Knife earns its keep. You notice, looking back down toward the river, that the faint gray-green tinge is visible even from here — a narrow, slow-moving tongue of it along the inside of the upstream bend, not diffusing the way runoff would. It holds its shape too well.

Huginn lands on a fence-post twenty feet off and regards the river for a long moment. Then it looks at you. It does not croak. The silence is the comment.

turns.summary: Silas resurrects a cleaned rabbit — not by understanding but by reaching past understanding — and the animal sits breathing in the wet grass, oriented toward Millbrook.
turns.narration:
You crouch over the nearer rabbit, its cleaned body cooling in the wet grass, and reach inward for the thread of meaning-sense that has never had a name — only the old feeling of the world going thin. The meadow gives you nothing back. Whatever Numen-Sense reads, it reads significance, and a dead rabbit in a spring field is only a dead rabbit: no weight, no pull, no thinness in the air around it. The ability slides off the moment like a hand on wet stone. The village hum below, the wrong color of the water, the unnamed door behind your life — none of it translates into the knowing you needed. You straighten, jaw tight, and let it go.

You look at Huginn. Huginn looks at you with the eye that is too calm, and it does not move. A long beat. Then it glides off the fence-post and drops low over the riverbank — not hunting, not pointing, just repositioning so that its shadow falls directly across the rabbit's body. It is not a gesture. It is not *meant* to be anything. But the shadow lands like a hand laid flat on a table to say: *here*.

You go back to the rabbit. You do not know what you are doing, exactly. This has never had a name either. You press two fingers to the animal's sternum — still warm, barely — and the thing that lives in the space between a carpenter's eye and a craftsman's patience uncoils. Something in the valley's old grain answers. The reek of metal off the river sharpens for one hard second, as if the air just drew breath, and then the rabbit flinches.

It is not a clean resurrection. The animal rolls upright with the lurching stiffness of something that has been somewhere and come back before it was ready — nose working, hind legs uncertain, sides moving in short quick breaths. Its eyes are clear. It orients south, toward the village, and sits there vibrating at the end of its own confusion. Huginn, from the fence-post it has returned to, croaks once. The croak lands on the word *well* — and then it looks away, and the word un-happens, and it is just a bird sound.

turns.summary: Order-Sense reads a structural seam in the resurrected rabbit — a second pattern woven through the original, stable but of unknown authorship.
turns.narration:
You kneel beside the rabbit and let Order-Sense open — not toward the water, not toward the meadow's hidden channels, but straight down into the animal itself. The ability settles into its reading mode the way a hand settles onto a familiar tool: structure, rules, load-bearing points.

What you find is not what you expected. The rabbit's systems are running — heart cycling, breath shallow but even, the small machinery of a living thing doing what living things do. But the structure has a seam in it. Not a wound, not damage. More like a splice. The original architecture — muscle, bone, nerve, the exact particular arrangement of this animal — is intact and load-bearing. What was added sits alongside it, not replacing anything, threading through the existing pattern like a second warp through a weave already set. You cannot read its source. Order-Sense reads structure, not intent, and the seam has no legible authorship. It is simply there, holding.

The rabbit blinks. One ear tilts toward the river. Its orientation hasn't shifted — still south, still toward Millbrook, with the quiet purposefulness of something that has been given a direction it doesn't understand. The seam in its structure is stable. Whatever the valley's old grain put into this animal, it isn't leaking, isn't failing. It is doing exactly what it was built to do. You just cannot read what that is.

Huginn has not moved from the fence-post. It is looking downriver with the studied indifference of something that has decided to have no opinion about what you just learned.

turns.summary: The tether to the resurrected rabbit fails — the connection was not Silas's to keep. He scouts the approaches to Millbrook on the walk back and enters the village lane.
turns.narration:
The rabbit is already moving when you try to hold it — not fleeing, just going, that quiet southward pull it came back with. You reach for the tether the way you reached for the resurrection itself: not with the named abilities, not with anything you can describe in plain terms, just the same deeper-than-sense place you found before. This time the meadow gives you nothing. The seam in the animal's structure, that second warp threaded through its bones, is simply not addressable from here. Whatever authorship wove it, it does not answer your reach. The connection you made in that one sharp second of metal-smell was not yours to keep — it was given, and the giving is done.

The rabbit reaches the sedge line and disappears into it, heading south toward the village fence line. Gone. Not dead — your gut reads that much — but gone from your sensing, as clean as a snuffed candle. Huginn watches the sedge close behind it, then watches you. Its silence has the particular quality of a creature deciding whether to comment.

You turn south and walk, using the transit back as cover to read the meadow's edges — Lightsense peeling the morning shadows, cataloging what is seen and what is not. The game trail through the sedge is empty. The slope above the terraced gardens is open and quiet. By the time you reach the village lane you have a reasonable picture: the dock-side is Millbrook's open face, fishermen and traders; the mill-road carries slow morning traffic, a carter and two women with loaded baskets; the elder hall sits closed but not locked; a storehouse on the south lane has a new padlock on a very old hasp. The Waterskin on your belt is a reminder that the well, not the river, is where people drink now.

Huginn lands on a fence-post at the lane's edge and performs the posture of a bird thinking about absolutely nothing at all. The pigeon is still with you, cleaned and cooling. You are a stranger coming in from the north meadow with a hunting knife and a game bird, which is a specific kind of readable.

turns.summary: Silas is turned away at the cookhouse — a stranger with strange company — though a boy in the back takes an interest.
turns.narration:
The cookhouse is a low building behind the well — smoke-stained, with a half-door propped open and the smell of rendered fat and yesterday's onions coming through it. A woman with flour on her wrists and an expression like a door that doesn't want to open stands behind the counter, already watching you approach. You set the pigeon on the counter. Cleaned, fresh, a decent bird.

She looks at it. Then at you. Her eyes go to Huginn on your shoulder — or wherever the bird has chosen to perch this moment — and something in her face closes the rest of the way. A stranger walking in off the south lane, uncanny company, dropping a dead bird on her prep surface. Whatever you hoped to open with, it isn't working. 'We've got our own fowl,' she says, flat, and doesn't reach for it. 'Leave it at the smokehouse if you want coin. I'm not running a trading post.'

She turns back to her work. The half-door is still open — you're not thrown out — but the conversation that would have come with a meal and a willing ear isn't on offer here. One other person is in the room: a boy, maybe twelve, sweeping the floor near the back wall. He glanced up at you when you came in. He's glancing up again now, with the particular attention children give to things adults refuse to look at.

turns.summary: Silas takes the pigeon to the smokehouse and meets the man working there.
turns.narration:
You pick the pigeon back up off the counter. The cookhouse woman's shoulders drop half an inch — relief, not disappointment. The boy in the back watches you tuck the bird under your arm, his broom going still.

The smokehouse is easy enough to find: follow the lane past the well, turn where the smell of hickory and rendered fat sharpens into something purposeful. It's a squat stone building with a low doorway and a wedge of smoke leaking from the eave-gap, thin and blue in the cold morning air. The door stands open. Inside, two broad racks of fish hang on iron hooks over a banked fire — river perch, by the look of them, a little gray at the gill, which explains the early nets.

A man in his late forties stands at a cutting block near the back wall, working a fillet knife with the economy of someone who has done this every morning of his life. He has the blocky forearms of a man who handles wet wood and heavy loads, and a canvas apron gone dark with use. He glances up when you step through the doorway. His eyes tick to Huginn — wherever the bird has settled — and then to the pigeon in your hand, in that order. The bird gets a longer look than the man does.

"Cookhouse send you?" he says. No warmth, no hostility. Just the flat factual register of someone who has a lot of fish to process and wants to know if your business is worth stopping for.

turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the smokehouse as a fragile fixed-account operation absorbing slow damage from the fish shortage — the pigeon has leverage.
turns.narration:
The smokehouse man is still watching you, fillet knife easy in his hand, waiting for a reason to either talk or get back to his fish.

You give him a small nod — *a moment* — and let Order-Sense open quietly, the way you'd ease a door rather than push it. No reach, no press. Just attention, going a little deeper than the eyes.

The structure resolves. The smokehouse is a load-bearing thing in a simple economy: one man, one fire, the village's protein through the shoulder months when the river gives less. The rack capacity says he processes more than he sells fresh — most of it goes preserved, distributed, probably on account. The wear on the cutting block is centered and even, which is the mark of someone who has done the same motion for years without variation. This is not a tradesman who experiments. He serves a list of regular customers, and the list is probably older than his apron.

The weight, though — the weight falls on the fish. Two racks of perch, gray-gilled, not bad enough to pull but not what a morning's work should look like. The fire is banked lower than the space needs, which means he's stretching the wood. Catches are down, quality is sliding, and a man who runs on accounts and regulars cannot easily absorb that kind of slow erosion.

The pigeon under your arm is suddenly more interesting than it was a minute ago.

"Cookhouse didn't send me," you say. "Came on my own. Two rabbits in the pack, this pigeon, all fresh this morning. Thought you might have more use for variety than your rack does right now."

turns.summary: Silas and Aldric the smokehouse man strike a provisioning deal — hunting in exchange for food and supplies, with a mutual interest in the river contamination.
turns.narration:
You set the pigeon on the cutting block — not as a donation, as an opening. "Fresh bird," you say. "Cleaned this morning. I'm in the area a few days, maybe more. I hunt well, and I'm heading north anyway. If you want, I keep bringing what I take — whatever the meadows give — and in return you help provision me. Meal and supplies where you can spare them."

The man's eyes move from the pigeon to you, doing the arithmetic. He turns the bird over with two fingers, checking the work. The cleaning is clean. He sets it down.

"North," he says. It's not quite a question. "River's running gray up that way. Wouldn't go far north without a reason." A beat. He's still holding the fillet knife, loose. "You mentioned keeping an eye on the catches." His chin tips toward the gray-gilled perch on the rack. "You see something up there worth seeing, I want to hear it. Same terms — you bring what you take, I keep you fed and supply what I can spare. No guarantees on quantity. The shortage hits my accounts too."

He picks up the pigeon, turns to the second rack, and makes space. "Name's Aldric," he says, without looking back. "You need a meal now or after?"

turns.summary: Silas hands over two rabbits and the pigeon; Aldric trades preserved food and names Mara Wells at the supply store for hunting kit.
turns.narration:
You unshoulder the pack and pull the two rabbits free — cleaned, tied at the hocks with a twist of cord, still cold from the morning. You lay them beside the pigeon on the cutting block. Aldric looks at the three of them together: the birds flanking the pair of rabbits, the arrangement tidy. His expression doesn't change much, but his knife hand relaxes another degree.

"Two rabbits and the pigeon," you say. "For preserved food — something that travels. And I've got a question alongside that, if you'll take it."

Aldric turns one rabbit over, checks the belly cut, nods once. The work is good and he can see it. He sets it back. "I can do dried perch — what's left of decent quality — and some salted pork. Small parcel, but it'll hold a week on the road." He moves to the far rack and starts pulling wrapped parcels from a shelf beneath it, not hurrying. "The question?"

"Hunting kit," you say. "Traps, snares — anything that helps me bring you more of this. Or a name, if you know one." Aldric sets a cloth-wrapped parcel on the block and tilts his head toward the village lane. "Mara Wells keeps the supply store — east side of the well, red door. She stocks rope, hardware, occasionally traps when someone trades one in. Tell her the smokehouse sent you and she won't charge you a finder's fee on top." He wraps a second parcel, smaller, and knots it. His eyes go to the rabbits again — already thinking ahead to the rack. "Snares you'll do better setting yourself. Deadfalls too. But Mara's your first stop."

turns.summary: Silas introduces himself properly; Aldric names the village blacksmith Pell on the south lane as the local source for heavy hunting hardware.
turns.narration:
You pick up the two cloth-wrapped parcels — solid weight, the perch smell faintly through the fabric — and tuck them into the pack. "Silas Weir," you say, settling the straps. "Appreciate the fair trade, Aldric."

He dips his chin — the acknowledgment of a man who doesn't waste ceremony. "Weir." He's already lifting the first rabbit toward the iron hook, orienting it by habit. "Bowyer's not in the village. Had one, moved on two winters back — nobody filled it." He loops the cord and steps back to check the hang. "Blacksmith you want is Pell. Works the forge on the south lane, past the elder's post. Does edge work, hardware, repairs. If you need heavier iron — snares, spear-tips, that kind of thing — she's your person." A pause, and he glances at Huginn, who has settled on the low beam above the door and is watching the rabbits go up with professional interest. "Your bird always watch the hang like that?"

Huginn does not move. Its eye catches the fire-glow from the banked coals and holds it.

"Mostly," you say.

Aldric makes a sound that isn't quite a laugh, picks up his fillet knife, and gets back to work — the conversation closed in the comfortable way of a man satisfied with what just happened, not the closed way of one who isn't.

turns.summary: Silas arrives at Pell's forge on the south lane and gets his first look at her stock.
turns.narration:
You step out of the smokehouse into the lane's morning light, Huginn dropping from the beam to your shoulder in one unhurried movement. The hickory smell follows you half a block, then gives way to woodsmoke and damp stone and the ordinary business of a village mid-morning. The main lane runs straight past the well — you can see the rope and bucket, a woman drawing water, two children arguing about something near the post — and then south, past a low building with an elder's mark on the doorframe, the lane narrows and the forge smell starts before the building is visible: hot iron, coal dust, the particular dry heat that comes off a working fire even through two feet of stone wall.

Pell's forge is a squat structure set back from the lane, the front half open under a wide overhang where the light is better for close work. The woman inside has her back to you when you arrive — she's running a file along something clamped in the vise, long strokes, steady pressure, not looking up. The shop front is organized the way a competent person organizes things: not pretty, but findable. Iron hooks in rows. Coils of heavier chain. A board hung with flat snare plates, toggle-springs, and the kind of anchoring stakes that go into root-tangled ground and stay there. On a lower shelf, three spear-tips in descending weight — the middle one broad-bladed, designed for boar or heavy game, the lightest a proper hunting point with a clean taper.

Huginn eyes the spear-tips with the patient interest it gives to most things. Pell finishes the stroke she was on, sets the file down, and turns — takes in the pack, the bird on your shoulder, and you, in that order.

turns.summary: Silas introduces himself to Pell at the south-lane forge — she takes him in order: gear, bird, man.
turns.narration:
She doesn't turn at the sound of your boots on the overhang stones, just keeps the file moving — long, even strokes, the metal giving a dry whisper with each pass. You set your pack down just inside the overhang's shade, and Huginn hops off your shoulder to the nearest iron hook-row, folding its wings with the dignity of someone who has found acceptable seating.

"Don't worry," you say, watching her work. "I took you in the same order — the quality gear, the long file strokes, and the curve of your bend." A beat. "Hi. I'm Silas."

The file stops. Not a flinch — just a clean halt, the way someone stops when they've decided to. She sets the file across the vise jaw and turns around in the unhurried way of someone who was already listening. Mid-forties, broad through the shoulders, soot in the creases of her knuckles. Her eyes go to Huginn first — the bird meets her gaze with the equanimity of something that has witnessed considerably worse than a forge — then to you, then to the pack at your feet. The whole read takes about three seconds.

"Pell," she says, and lets that sit a moment. "Aldric send you, or are you just finding the smoke?"

turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the forge — snare stock is depleting, mid-weight spear-tip has had hands on it repeatedly, edge work is the shop's real load.
turns.narration:
You don't answer right away. Instead you let your attention settle — not on Pell but through her, past the turned face and into the shop's structure beneath. Order-Sense comes up quiet and clear, the way it does when a system is well-maintained: you feel the forge's logic in a breath.

The spear-tips are the leading edge. Three grades, all accounted for, none of them moving fast — the heavy boar-point has a thin coat of oil that says it's been sitting, but the mid-weight shows recent handling on the tang, a worn patch where someone kept picking it up and setting it back down. Somebody is thinking about buying it and hasn't. The snare hardware is different: the toggle-springs on the board are down to four pairs, and the anchor stakes have gaps in the row where a full set used to hang. That stock is depleting and not being replaced at pace — either the supplier is slow or Pell hasn't ordered, which means either coin or materials, not demand. The chain coils are full. The file on the vise jaw is a third through its working life. The forge itself is burning efficiently, nothing wasted.

What she's prioritizing: edge work and repairs. What's scarce: snare hardware. Where the pressure is: the mid-weight spear-tip, the one someone keeps reconsidering.

You look back at her. "Aldric sent me," you say. "Though I'd have found the smoke on my own eventually."

turns.summary: Silas charms Pell by admiring her craft and asking if she hunts — the door opens.
turns.narration:
You let the Order-Sense reading settle and meet her eyes directly. "But Aldric sent me for the hardware," you say, "and I came for the woman who makes it." You gesture at the board — the snare plates, the toggle-springs, the anchor stakes with their visible gaps — and then back to the vise, the file, the workpiece still clamped mid-job. "You run a tidy shop. Everything in its place, stock you can account for at a glance. I find that genuinely impressive."

Pell's expression doesn't shift much, but something in her posture does — a slight unfolding, the way a person eases when they've been read accurately rather than flattered. She picks the file back up and turns it once in her hand, not resuming work, just holding it.

"Aldric," she says, flat, confirming rather than asking. Then: "Do you hunt?"

"Looking to," you say. "North meadows, mostly. Maybe further upstream if the trails hold." A pause. "Do you?"

She glances at Huginn, who has been watching her with the particular patience of something that has seen a great deal more than it mentions. "Used to," Pell says. "Before the forge had enough work to make it a second job." Her eyes come back to you. "Snare hardware. Chain. What weight of tip are you after?" She's not warm, exactly — but the door is open now, and she walked to it herself.

turns.summary: Silas offers forge-help in exchange for hunting company — Pell considers it as a squall blows through and clears.
turns.narration:
"I get it — there's a lot of work to do." You glance at the vise, the clamped piece, the backlog of small jobs visible on the side table. "If I help you out, do you think you'd have time to come hunting up north with me?"

Pell looks at you steadily for a moment. Not calculating — she did that already, when you walked in. She's just letting the offer land. Then the first real weather of the morning announces itself: a squall blows through the south lane without much warning, kicking grit off the lane stones and rattling the iron hook-row where Huginn sits. The bird doesn't move. Rain comes in three hard seconds of percussion on the overhang roof, then stops entirely, leaving the air sharp and washed and smelling of cold stone and green things somewhere uphill. A short gust scatters ash from the forge-mouth across the floor.

Pell watches the squall pass the same way she watches everything — without hurry. Then she crouches, sweeps the ash back with one boot toward the fire-grate, and straightens. "Help out." She repeats it flatly, not mocking, just tasting the shape of it. "I've got a backlog on edge work and the snare hardware needs restocking before the north meadow runs get worth anything." She looks at the side table, then back at you. "You know your way around a forge?"

Huginn shifts on the hook-row, settling its wings with that particular weighted patience that means it has decided the current situation is sufficiently stable to observe from a distance. Outside, a thin clear cold has replaced the squall — the lane stones are dark with brief rain, and the light has that early-spring quality of having been freshly decided. It's a good morning to be inside a warm shop, which is probably not lost on either of you.

turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the backlog pile cold — Silas names every fault correctly, including the sprung collar on the froe, and Pell accepts the deal.
turns.narration:
Pell's question hangs in the forge-warm air. You don't answer it directly — not yet.

Your eyes move to the side table, the backlog pile, and you let Order-Sense come up the way you breathe: quiet, no effort wasted. The stack resolves itself in a few seconds of looking. A drawknife with a rolled edge — someone used it on green wood, probably wet wood, and the bevel gave. A billhook that needs the whole face reground. Two skinning blades, both sound but dull, work that takes an hour and a steady hand rather than skill. A hand-axe with a hairline in the eye — that one's tricky, needs the eye dressed before anything touches the edge or you'll chase the fault forever. And at the bottom, half-buried, a froe that's been slightly sprung at the collar, something a wright would spot faster than a smith might.

You look back at Pell. "Drawknife with a rolled edge from wet wood. Billhook wants the whole face. Two skinners — dull, nothing wrong underneath. Hand-axe with a fault in the eye that needs dressing before the blade. Froe's sprung at the collar." You let a beat pass. "I know my way around a forge well enough to clear that backlog without supervision. I won't touch anything I'm not sure of."

Huginn shifts on the hook-row, a single claw-tap on iron that might be punctuation.

Pell turns the file over once in her hand. She looks at the pile, then at you, with the expression of someone who just watched a stranger name their dog's middle name without being told. "The froe," she says, "most people call it bent." A pause, flat and appraising. "Collar-sprung is the right word for it." She sets the file on the vise jaw. "All right. Backlog first. We talk hunting when it's clear."

turns.summary: Silas settles into Pell's backlog — drawknife bevel corrected, trust quietly building.
turns.narration:
You pull the drawknife from the pile and hold it edge-up in the forge light. The damage is exactly what you saw: a rolled bevel, not cracked, just persuaded wrong by wet wood and impatience. Recoverable in twenty minutes if you don't rush it.

Pell has gone back to her own vise-work without ceremony, which is its own kind of trust. You find the sharpening station — a decent flat stone, a slip, a leather strop hanging from a peg — and settle into the rhythm. Grip the handles, feel the bevel angle, let the stone do the thinking. The forge pops once behind you. Huginn shifts on the hook-row, claws ticking iron, and watches the lane with the patient attention of something that has nowhere else to be.

The work is simple and you are good at it. The rolled edge comes back in stages — first the gross correction, the stone cutting away the folded-over lip, then finer passes until the bevel is clean and consistent from heel to heel. You test the edge against your thumbnail: it bites without skating. Another few passes on the strop and it will be ready to cut wood again. Pell glances over once, notes the angle you're holding, and returns to her file without comment. That's the whole review.

The billhook is next on the pile. Outside, the lane stones are already drying in the spring air, the squall gone like it was never there.

turns.summary: Billhook work underway; Pell names Corvin — a farmer's son who keeps handling the mid-weight spear-tip without buying.
turns.narration:
The billhook comes next. You clamp it in the spare vise — the face is worn to a convex hollow, somebody's been treating it like an axe rather than a blade — and you take the bastard file to it in long, even strokes, letting the metal tell you where it wants to go. The rhythm settles, and after a few minutes you speak into it without breaking stride.

"The mid-weight spear-tip on the lower shelf." You don't look up. "Someone's been at it enough to rub the oil thin on the socket, but they're not buying. Seems like a specific kind of wanting."

Pell is quiet for a beat — the sound of her file running across whatever she's dressing in the vise. Then: "Boy named Corvin. Farms east furlong with his father. Comes in every few days, picks it up, puts it down. Tips over his head right now — can't afford the mid-weight, and the light point won't do what he needs for boar." A pause. "Or what he thinks he needs. Probably doesn't know yet that he doesn't know."

Huginn makes a short sound from the hook-row — not quite a croak, more a dry tick, like a knuckle on hollow wood. Pell doesn't look at the bird, but her filing slows for half a second before it resumes. You lift the billhook, sight along the face: flat now, the hollow chased out. You move to the slip stone and start on the edge.

turns.summary: Silas strips his shirt in the forge-warmth and proposes a modified light spear-tip that might actually serve Corvin's boar-hunting ambitions.
turns.narration:
The billhook is as good as it's going to get without a full re-face, so you set the file down, pull it from the vise, and tug your shirt off over your head in one motion — the kind of casual efficiency that belongs to someone who's worked enough hot shops to stop caring about formality. You drape the shirt over the end of the sharpening station and pick the slip stone up again. The forge is warm enough to earn it.

Huginn makes no comment. Pell doesn't look up, but she doesn't not look, either — a craftsman's peripheral awareness, the same sense she'd use to track where an apprentice's hands are near a fire. You start the edge work on the billhook's curved face and keep your voice easy.

"What's Corvin actually hunting?"

A pause. Pell's file keeps its rhythm. "Says boar. Heard there's a sounder working the north wood, wants to be the one who does something about it."

"Mid-weight point won't stop a boar at the shoulder." You tilt the blade to check the edge in the forge light. "But a light point isn't all wrong if the geometry's modified — socket sleeve thickened, spine reinforced behind the socket so the shaft doesn't torque. He'd need the right shaft length and the nerve to use the throat rather than going through the side." You let that sit. "That work you could do?"

Now she does turn — quarter-turn, the minimum. Her eyes move from the billhook to your hands to your face, in that order. "I could do it. Collar and sleeve work, yes. Whether the boy has the nerve is a separate question." She turns back. "And whether he has the coin for the modification on top of the point."

turns.summary: Pell agrees to the three-way hunt arrangement — modification at cost, no margin, on the condition Silas keeps Corvin from doing something permanent.
turns.narration:
You work the billhook's curved face with the slip stone while you lay it out — not a pitch, just a practical arrangement stated plainly. You'll cover the modification cost on the light point. You bring the finished piece to Corvin before the hunt, on the road north. Pell comes along, Corvin gets his spear and his adventure, and you get decent company in the north wood. Three people who know what they're doing are better than one person who doesn't, against a sounder.

Pell is quiet for long enough that you hear the froe shift in its pile. Then she sets her file down on the anvil ledge — not put away, just paused — and looks at you directly for the first time since you started the drawknife.

"Corvin doesn't know what he doesn't know about boar," she says. The tone is not unkind. It's the same register she'd use to say the bevel is wrong. "That's the problem. You take him north with a point he just received this morning, he'll be brave about it in the wrong moment."

She picks the file back up. Works two strokes. "But someone capable is beside him, that changes the math." Another stroke. "You cover the modification, I supply it at cost. No margin — that's what it's worth if you're also keeping the boy from doing something permanent." A quarter-turn glance, the same one she gave your file angle. "I'll walk north with you. He can carry his own spear."

turns.summary: Both skinners brought to a clean edge; Silas settles into the backlog rhythm, hand-axe and froe still waiting.
turns.narration:
The two skinners are next. You pick up the first one and find the edge with your thumb — not dull so much as rolled, the kind of wear that comes from someone stropping against the grain — and you set it bevel-down on the flat stone with a grin you don't quite suppress. Three people. A blacksmith who used to hunt, a farmer's son who wants to prove something, and you. The north wood is going to be interesting.

Pell doesn't notice the grin, or notices and files it away. She's back at her own vise, the file moving in the short, precise strokes of someone addressing a specific problem rather than running a general course. Her shoulders are set square and her left hand braces with the kind of practiced tension that doesn't waste effort — years of forge-habit in the geometry of it. You clock it the way you'd clock any good technique: once, honestly, then back to your own work.

The first skinner comes right with two passes on the stone and three on the strop. You hold it up, catch the light from the open front of the shop, tilt it until the bevel shows clean. Good. You set it on the bench and pick up the second. The rhythm is easy now — the slip stone, the strop, the quarter-turn check. Huginn shifts on the hook-row, one foot to the other, watching something on the lane that apparently concludes to its satisfaction. It settles.

The hand-axe is going to be the interesting one. You can already see the hairline in the eye from here, and you leave it for last, working the second skinner down to a clean edge while the forge warmth does its job on your back. The arrangement sits comfortable in your chest — not a prize, just a thing that came together the way good work sometimes does, one honest assessment meeting another.

turns.summary: Pell describes the north wood from memory — wet ground, a granite shelf, boar likely in the lower section.
turns.narration: The hand-axe can wait another few minutes. You're working the second skinner along the flat stone when you pitch your voice across the shop...

turns.summary: Pell maps the north wood from memory — solo hunting, the wet lower section, the granite shelf as a natural backstop.
turns.narration:
You set the second skinner on the bench and look over at Pell, keeping your voice easy — the question landing between strokes of her file, not against them.

She answers without stopping work. "Lower section, mostly. Where the ground goes soft from the slope springs — you can read the trails better there, soil takes a print." A pause while she repositions the piece in the vise. "Alone, those years. Forge wasn't busy enough to need a second hand, so I took a bow out Thursday mornings when the light was right." The file resumes. "Deer, mostly. A few good days on turkey. Went as far north as that granite shelf I mentioned — twice, maybe three times. Ate lunch up there and came back down the east trail."

Huginn shifts on the hook-row, one foot to the other, and Pell glances at the bird without comment — the third time she's done that, you've noticed, but she's never addressed it.

"Alone," she says again, and it doesn't sound like a complaint or a boast, just a fact she's accounting for. "Different thing, three of us going in with a spear. The lower section will hear us coming. Boar sounder's probably bedded in the wet ground by the springs — that's where the rooting's best. We push north quiet and let the shelf work as a backstop if something goes wrong."

turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the hand-axe hairline — an old stop-crack in the eye wall, not in the weld; needs a heat-collar before edge work.
turns.narration:
You set the second skinner aside and pick up the hand-axe. It's a good weight — the head still true, the handle seasoned hardwood, iron poll polished from years of palm-work. The hairline is visible only at a certain angle, a thin shadow running from the back of the eye toward the cheek.

You let Order-Sense in quietly, the way you'd hold a door open just enough to read the room beyond. The axe's structure resolves — the grain of the iron, the weld seam from the eye's original shaping, the load paths that run through the head when it meets wood. The hairline sits in the eye wall, not the cheek. It hasn't propagated into the weld, and it hasn't reached the bit. The crack is old — winter cold most likely, maybe one hard knock against a frozen post — and it stopped when the stress did. The iron around it is sound.

You turn the axe once in your hands and set it back on the bench. Not a grinding job. Not even a wedge-set. The eye wall needs a brace-wrap and a heat-collar to keep the crack from walking any further, and then the edge work can proceed without risk. You glance toward Pell's vise. "Hand-axe hairline is in the eye wall — old stop-crack, hasn't moved into the weld. It needs a heat-collar before I put edge on it. You have bar stock light enough for a wrap?"

turns.summary: Froe collar reset and seated; backlog cleared except the hand-axe waiting on bar stock for its heat-collar.
turns.narration:
The hand-axe goes back on the bench with a light click, out of the way of the sharpening station but within reach when Pell wants it. You tell her the crack's old and stopped — heat-collar on the eye wall before any edge work, and you'll need a strip of bar stock for it. She makes a sound that means *noted* and reaches past the vise for a short length of flat iron without being asked.

The froe is last in the pile for a reason. You work the collar free with the belt knife's spine and two fingers, checking the set of the sprung ring against the blade's shoulder. It's not broken — the iron stretched rather than cracked, which means the fix is a careful squeeze in the vise and a reset of the collar's seat, not a re-forge. Pell's vise is a good one. You crank it down slow, feeling for the give, and the collar closes back to its proper line with a short complaint of metal. You dress the seat with three strokes of the file, check the blade doesn't rock, and set the froe on the bench beside the billhook.

Structural work: done. The rack holds the drawknife, billhook, both skinners, and the froe — all clean. The hand-axe sits apart, waiting on its heat-collar. You straighten, roll your shoulder once, and look across the shop at Pell. Huginn shifts on the hook-row and resettles, one talon curling around the iron like punctuation.

"Collar piece," you say to Pell, meaning the bar stock for the axe. It's the one question still open between you and a clear backlog.

turns.summary: Bar stock confirmed — Pell supplies a clean strip for the hand-axe heat-collar; the backlog is fully within reach of completion.
turns.narration:
Pell sets the flat iron on the bench between you and taps it once with her knuckle — a short strip, maybe nine inches, quarter-inch thickness. 'That'll collar it,' she says, and goes back to the vise.

You pick it up and turn it in the light from the overhang. The grain is clean, no laminations, good working temperature from sitting near the forge. You hold it against the eye wall of the hand-axe — the fit is workable; you'll need to bring the strip to a slight curve before seating it, but the stock is right. You tell her so.

'Then set it when you're ready.' She doesn't look up. 'Fire's banked. Take what heat you need.'

Huginn shifts on the hook-row — one talon, then the other — and resettles with the unhurried patience of something that has watched many small problems get solved and found none of them surprising. The forge breathes its low steady heat. The hand-axe question is answered; the backlog, all of it, is now within reach of being closed.

lastTurn.narration:
Pell sets the flat iron on the bench between you and taps it once with her knuckle — a short strip, maybe nine inches, quarter-inch thickness. 'That'll collar it,' she says, and goes back to the vise.

You pick it up and turn it in the light from the overhang. The grain is clean, no laminations, good working temperature from sitting near the forge. You hold it against the eye wall of the hand-axe — the fit is workable; you'll need to bring the strip to a slight curve before seating it, but the stock is right. You tell her so.

'Then set it when you're ready.' She doesn't look up. 'Fire's banked. Take what heat you need.'

Huginn shifts on the hook-row — one talon, then the other — and resettles with the unhurried patience of something that has watched many small problems get solved and found none of them surprising. The forge breathes its low steady heat. The hand-axe question is answered; the backlog, all of it, is now within reach of being closed.
lastTurn.sceneSummary: Bar stock confirmed — Pell supplies a clean strip for the hand-axe heat-collar; the backlog is fully within reach of completion.

choices.label: Work the collar now — bring the strip to heat, curve it, and seat it on the eye wall. Close the backlog entirely.
Hand-axe is in good shape and you know the fix — ask Pell now if she knows where Corvin's east furlong is. Start pulling the hunt party together while the iron's still warm.
Use Order-Sense on the bar stock itself before you collar it — read the strip's load structure and make sure the heat-curve will seat true.

Set the collar work aside for a moment and step to the shop's open front — look out at the lane and take the morning's measure. Who's moving, what's the river light doing, is anything different today.

scene.setting: Pell's forge, south lane of Millbrook — open-front stone building under a wide work overhang. Spring mid-morning light through the overhang opening. Forge banked low, heat steady.

npcsPresent.name: Pell
npcsPresent.state: Back at the vise, file resumed, not watching — content the bar-stock question is settled.

npcsPresent.name: Huginn
npcsPresent.state: Resettled on the hook-row, both talons settled, watching the shop with its usual unhurried attention.

Billhook — face-work complete, on the rack
Two skinning blades — sharpened, on the rack
Froe — collar reset and seated, on the rack
Hand-axe — hairline in eye wall (stop-crack, old); heat-collar needed; bar stock now confirmed and in hand
Bar stock strip — nine inches, quarter-inch, clean grain; in Silas's hand
Sharpening station: flat stone, slip, leather strop
Iron hooks in rows along the shop wall
Coils of heavier chain (full)
Board with snare plates, toggle-springs (four pairs remaining), and anchor stakes
Three spear-tips on the lower shelf — heavy boar-point (oiled), mid-weight (oiled), light point
threads: Heat-collar on the hand-axe — bar stock in hand, forge banked and ready; one task remaining to fully close the backlog
Corvin still to be found and invited — east furlong, not yet contacted
Hunt departs once backlog is fully clear

quests.title: Meat for the Smokehouse
quests.summary: Hunt the meadows north of Millbrook and bring Aldric what you take — he feeds you and supplies what he can spare. He wants to know anything you find that explains the declining catches.
progress: Deal struck on World-day 12, morning. Aldric has his eye on the gray-gilled perch and wants real information about the upstream situation.
Silas delivered two rabbits and the pigeon — first hunting delivery made. Aldric provided preserved trail food in return and directed Silas to Mara Wells for hunting equipment.
Silas has arrived at Pell's forge — the next step in equipping for sustained hunting north of the village.
Silas has offered forge-labor to Pell in exchange for hunting company north of the village — negotiation ongoing at the forge.
Pell accepted the deal — backlog labor in exchange for hunting company north of the village. Terms agreed verbally; work starts now.
Backlog work begun at Pell's forge — drawknife bevel corrected, trust accumulating. Hunting company north will be available once the pile is clear.
Pell agreed to join the north-wood hunt at cost — modification on the light point covered by Silas, Corvin invited as a third. Hunt party of three forming; terms set.

cookhouse-woman.name: Cookhouse Woman
cookhouse-woman.description: Flour-wrists, flat affect, door-that-doesn't-want-to-open energy — runs the cookhouse behind the well and doesn't trust strangers with strange birds.
history: [d1] Refused the pigeon and the implied trade. Turned back to her work. Not hostile, just closed.
knownFacts: Wary of Silas specifically — the uncanny company (Huginn) registered visibly
Directed him to the smokehouse for game trades
cookhouse-woman.statusNote: At the cookhouse behind the well, working prep, conversation closed.

cookhouse-boy.name: Boy (name unknown)
cookhouse-boy.role: Sweeping the cookhouse, possibly cookhouse-woman's son or apprentice
cookhouse-boy.description: Maybe twelve, broom in hand — giving Silas the kind of attention children give to things adults refuse to look at.
knownFacts: Noticed Silas and Huginn where the woman would not engage
cookhouse-boy.statusNote: In the back of the cookhouse, sweeping, watching Silas.

aldric.name: Aldric
aldric.description: A blocky man in his late forties — fillet knife, dark apron, the flat practical register of someone who has worked the same fire for twenty years. Runs the village's preserved-fish distribution on fixed accounts; absorbed the catch decline
history: [d1] Clocked Huginn first, then the pigeon — asked if the cookhouse sent Silas, waiting for a reason to engage
[d1] Order-Sense read the operation around him — fixed accounts, regular customers, the weight falling on fish quality; he's absorbing slow loss from the catch decline. Silas named hims
[d1] You know this person's name now: Aldric (was "Smokehouse Man")
[d1] Accepted Silas's trade offer without ceremony — provisioning in exchange for hunting, with an explicit ask for information about the upstream situation. Named himself. Made space f
[d1] Accepted two rabbits and the pigeon — confirmed the work was good. Provided a parcel of dried perch and salted pork. Directed Silas to Mara Wells (red door, east of the well) for t
[d1] Silas gave his name; Aldric received it with a chin-dip and named Pell the blacksmith (south lane, past the elder's post) as the village source for heavier iron — snare hardware, s
[d1] Silas invoked his name to confirm the referral to Pell — name landed clean.
knownFacts: Gray-gilled perch suggest quality has been sliding for some time, not just today
He's aware the gray-green water comes from upstream and has been watching it
He runs fixed accounts — the catch decline is already hitting his regular customers
Mara Wells runs the village supply store — east side of the well, red door — and stocks rope, hardware, and occasional traded-in traps
Telling Mara 'the smokehouse sent you' waives her finder's fee
No bowyer currently in Millbrook — position unfilled since two winters ago
Pell is the village blacksmith, works the forge on the south lane past the elder's post — does edge work, hardware, repairs, heavier iron hunting kit
aldric.statusNote: At the smokehouse, back to filleting after the delivery.

pell.name: Pell
pell.role: Village blacksmith, Millbrook — south lane forge
pell.description: A working blacksmith running a tidy, unsentimental shop — file in hand, back to the door until the work is done. Practical organizer; reacts to competence over charm.
history: [d1] Silas named every fault in the backlog correctly — including 'collar-sprung' on the froe, which most people miss. She accepted the deal: backlog labor first, hunting discussion whe
[d1] Returned to her own vise-work without ceremony after Silas began the backlog — one glance at his angle, no comment. Baseline trust registered through silence.
[d1] Named Corvin unprompted — a farmer's son from the east furlong who keeps picking up the mid-weight spear-tip and putting it down. Offered the information without ceremony, filed th
[d1] Answered Corvin's situation plainly — the boy wants boar, the light point won't serve, the modification is within her skill but coin is the obstacle. Gave Silas a quarter-turn look
[d1] Agreed to the three-way hunt arrangement — modification on the light point at cost, no margin, on the condition a capable person is beside Corvin in the north wood. Committed to wa
[d1] Working her own vise in comfortable parallel — the arrangement settled, no need to manage it.
[d1] Described the north wood from memory — wet ground in the lower section, a granite shelf a quarter-mile north of the fence line where game trails run east-west. Warned plainly that
[d1] Described her north-wood hunting history — solo, lower section, wet ground by the slope springs, turkey and deer. Got as far as the granite shelf two or three times. Noted the east
[d1] Silas diagnosed the hand-axe hairline via Order-Sense and called the fix correctly — heat-collar on the eye wall. Question of bar stock now open.
[d1] Silas set the hand-axe aside with a clear diagnosis and note on the bar stock needed; structural backlog otherwise cleared. She reached for flat iron without being asked when the c
knownFacts: Stopped hunting the north meadows four to five years ago when the forge had enough work
The north wood's lower section stays wet from slope-fed springs — prime rooting ground for boar
A granite shelf sits about a quarter-mile north of the fence line; game trails run east-west along it, not north-south
Hunted the north wood alone, on Thursday mornings when forge work was slow
Stayed mostly to the lower section where soil takes a print — good trail-reading
Reached the granite shelf two or three times; used the east trail to come back down
The hand-axe hairline is an old stop-crack in the eye wall — hasn't reached the weld or the bit; needs a heat-collar before edge work proceeds
Froe collar was stretched not cracked — reset in the vise, not re-forged; Silas handled it without prompting
pell.statusNote: At her forge on the south lane — vise paused, waiting on the bar-stock answer.

notes: [d1] The smokehouse is a squat stone building just off the main lane past the well — hickory smoke, iron racks, banked fire. Operated by a blocky man in his forties. The morning rack holds gray-gilled rive

[d1] Pell the blacksmith operates a forge on the south lane, past the elder's post — does edge work, hardware, repairs, and heavier iron for hunting. The village has had no bowyer for two winters.
[d1] Pell's forge visited on World-day 12 — stocks snare hardware (plates, toggles, stakes), chain, and three grades of spear-tip. Open-front under a wide overhang, south lane past the elder's post.
[d1] A brief squall passed through on World-day 12 mid-morning — lane stones briefly wet, air sharp and cold after. Cleared fast.

upper-meadow.note: Site of the resurrection and subsequent Order-Sense reading — the seam in the rabbit's structure is now an established f

cookhouse.note: Low smoke-stained building behind the well — flour, rendered fat, half-door. Operator is closed to strangers; a boy swee

news.text: The Water Crisis stands at Discoloration across the valley: The Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings. Fish catches are down. Communities murmur but cooperate.

Word reaches you: Millbrook's elder panel formally accepted the evidence of an operating Precursor system beneath the valley — the first official governance body in the valley to name it publicly. (near millbrook)

Word reaches you: Usnea proposed leading a multi-settlement expedition into the Disputed Zone before Millbrook's assembled elder panel — the room could not follow; the gap between evidence and action named openly. (near

Word reaches you: Word of Millbrook's elder decision — and Fendt's public naming — reached the Radiant Plateau Edge District by Day 11 night via the eastern path. Fendt's name appeared in red chalk on the district acco (

Word reaches you: A filtration array supervisor marked their own subordinate — Fendt — on the Edge District accountability board and filed no accompanying record. A keeper of thirty years has never seen this before. (nea

This reaches your area — The lower filtration ledger — containing three weeks of falsified purity readings under Fendt's initials — has been removed from the array-office in the Edge District. (near radiant plateau edge)

form: Pale slight framed Necromancer - handsome intensity in the eyes and the hold of the clutching hand - green/blue power emanating from him with his undead Raven on his shoulder.

bio.motivation: You took the short way, and the short way was wrong. The air here tastes of metal and the light falls at an angle the sun cannot account for. There is a door in the hillside that was not on any map, and behind you the path you walked in on is no longer there.
bio.story: Their first day in the world was the thin place — it is a symbol before it is a door. read what it means; ask him what it is like. You finished something. Completely. It nearly got you exiled, and you are still not sure they were wrong.

water-crisis-discoloration.label: The River's Discoloration
facts: [d1] The Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings in Millbrook — observed firsthand at the dock. A fisherman pulled his nets in early. The discoloration appears to drift in slow wreaths from upstream. Fi

[d1] From the upper meadow north of Millbrook, the gray-green tinge is visible holding its shape inside the upstream bend — it does not diffuse the way runoff would. It moves with too much coherence to be

[d1] Aldric at the smokehouse has independently noted the upstream source — the gray-gilled perch confirm the catch has been compromised for some time, not just recently. He's watching and wants real infor

fendt-falsified-ledger.label: Fendt and the Falsified Ledger
facts: [d1] Someone named Fendt had their name marked in red chalk on the Radiant Plateau Edge District accountability board by their own supervisor. Three weeks of falsified water-purity readings under Fendt's i

millbrook.label: Millbrook
facts: [d1] Millbrook's elder panel formally accepted evidence of an operating Precursor system beneath the valley — the first official governance body in the valley to name it publicly. A figure named Usnea prop

[d1] The cookhouse woman's wariness at a stranger with uncanny company suggests Millbrook's openness to outsiders is thinner than the village's unaligned reputation implies — at least at ground level, on t

[d1] The smokehouse operates off the main lane past the well — morning fillet work, hickory smoke. The morning rack already holds gray-gilled river perch, confirming the fish catch is compromised even befo

[d1] The smokehouse operates on fixed accounts — preserved fish distributed to regular customers on a list older than the operator's apron. One man, one fire, no experimentation. The fish shortage is alrea
[d1] No bowyer in the village — the last one left two winters back, position unfilled. The blacksmith is Pell, south lane past the elder's post: edge work, hardware, snare iron, spear-tips.

silas-background-gift.label: The Unnamed Gift
facts: [d1] Silas resurrected a cleaned rabbit in the upper meadow — not through any known ability, but through something prior to understanding. The valley's old grain answered. The mechanism is unknown even to

[d1] An attempt to maintain a tether to the resurrected rabbit after release failed cleanly — the seam in the animal is not addressable from outside. Whatever authored the second pattern does not answer Si

huginn.label: Huginn
facts: [d1] Positioned its shadow over the dead rabbit at the moment Silas reached for the gift — not deliberate, perhaps, but load-bearing. Croaked one word ('well') and retracted it immediately.

rabbit-structural-seam.label: The Seam in the Returned Animal
facts: [d1] Order-Sense reveals the resurrected rabbit has two woven structures: its original intact architecture, and a second pattern threaded through it like a second warp. The seam is stable and load-bearing.

aldric.label: Aldric
facts: [d1] Smokehouse operator and village provisioner — runs fixed accounts for preserved fish, has noticed the upstream contamination, wants information about its cause. Struck a provisioning deal with Silas o

water-keeper.label: Mara Wells
facts: [d1] Runs the village supply store east of the well — red door. Stocks rope, hardware, and occasional traded-in traps. Telling her the smokehouse sent you avoids a finder's fee. Named by Aldric as the firs

pell.label: Pell
facts: [d1] Village blacksmith at the south-lane forge — stocks snare hardware, chain, and hunting spear-tips in three weights. Practical and organized; named by Aldric as the source for heavier hunting iron.
[d1] Responds to a self-deprecating opener with dry patience — gave her name without ceremony once she decided to engage. Clocked Huginn without fuss. Asked directly whether Aldric sent Silas.

[d1] Order-Sense reads her shop: snare hardware is the scarce stock — toggle-springs down to four pairs, anchor stakes with visible gaps. Edge work and repairs are her real load. The mid-weight spear-tip h
[d1] Used to hunt the north meadows until the forge had enough work to make it a second job — there's a former hunter's knowledge still in her.
[d1] Has an edge-work backlog and needs snare hardware restocked before north meadow runs are worth attempting — offered a labor-for-hunting-company deal in response to Silas's proposal.
[d1] Accepts competence as currency — naming the froe's sprung collar (not just 'bent') closed the deal. Backlog labor in exchange for hunting company north of the village, terms agreed.
[d1] Order-Sense used on the hand-axe in Pell's forge — hairline is a stop-crack in the eye wall, not the weld; requires a heat-collar before edge work. Pell paused her own work to hear the read.

corvin.label: Corvin
facts: [d1] A farmer's son from Millbrook's east furlong — visits Pell's forge repeatedly to handle the mid-weight spear-tip without buying. Can't afford it; light point won't serve what he has in mind (boar). Pe

[d1] Corvin wants to take a boar from a sounder reportedly working the north wood — his repeated handling of the mid-weight spear-tip confirms the ambition. The light point won't stop a boar at the shoulde

[d1] Pell named a specific concern: Corvin will be brave in the wrong moment. She agreed to supply the spear modification at cost only because a capable person will be beside him. His location: east furlon

millbrook-north-wood-boar.label: Boar Sounder — North Wood
facts: [d1] A sounder of boar has been spotted working the north wood above Millbrook. Corvin from the east furlong has heard of it and wants to act on it. Relevant to any hunting trip north of the village.

north-wood.label: The North Wood
facts: [d1] Pell's memory: dense in the middle, wet lower ground fed by slope springs (prime boar territory), a granite shelf a quarter-mile north of the fence line where game trails run east-west. The granite sh

[d1] Pell hunted the lower north wood solo for several years — wet ground from slope springs, best trail-reading in the valley floor section. A granite shelf sits roughly a quarter-mile north of the fence

gallery.prompt: A lean man kneeling in cold wet meadow grass, two fingers pressed to the chest of a dead rabbit. A large grey-black bird watches from a fence-post, its shadow falling precisely across the animal. The
gallery.caption: Silas resurrects a cleaned rabbit — not by understanding but by reaching past understandin

establishedFacts.text: Silas arrived in Millbrook on World-day 12 as a complete stranger — no reputation, no contacts, pack still on his back.
Silas Weir raised a cleaned, dead rabbit back to life in Millbrook's upper meadow on World-day 12 — no ritual, no understood mechanism; the valley's old grain answered something in him. The animal is

Silas struck a provisioning deal with Aldric the smokehouse man on World-day 12 — hunting in exchange for food and supplies, with Aldric asking for any information Silas finds about the upstream conta
Silas made his first hunting delivery to Aldric on World-day 12 — two rabbits and the pigeon. Aldric provided preserved trail food in return. The provisioning arrangement is now active.
Silas struck a labor deal with Pell the blacksmith — clearing her edge-work backlog in exchange for hunting company north of the village. Work begins now.
Pell agreed to join a three-way boar hunt in the north wood — modification on the light spear-tip at cost (covered by Silas), Corvin to be invited. Hunt departs once the forge backlog is clear.
